bins.name: Need for uniform national standards
bins.desc: ON supports codifying NERC Level 3 Alert recommendations and NOGRR 282 into uniform, enforceable national rules. The company argues this approach prevents regional fragmentation and ensures consistent technical requirements across all RTOs and ISOs.
quotes: ON strongly supports the effort by the Commission to consider potential reforms to large load interconnection.
Often overlooked are the power quality and system stability risks posed by these loads that are larger, more volatile, and more fragile than what grid operators are accustomed to seeing.

ON urges the Commission to use this and related rulemakings to accelerate adoption of new standards for the interconnection of large computational loads to ensure grid reliability, power quality, and system stability.
The NERC alert provides a strong initial set of actions and should be codified into uniform, enforceable rules.

ERCOT's large-load interconnection process for load facilities of 75 MW or greater, together with the pending NOGRR 282 and its associated work streams, establishes voltage and frequency ride-through requirements for Large Electronic Loads with explicit ramp-rate limits and addresses modeling, ramp behavior, operating coordination, and interconnection documentation.

The risk of inaction is regional fragmentation: PJM, the California Independent System Operator (CAISO), the Midcontinent Independent System Operator (MISO), the Southwest Power Pool (SPP), and other grid operators each developing their own responses.

bins.name: Compliance feasibility and cost
bins.desc: ON asserts that compliance with stringent large-load standards is achievable without significant project delays or cost increases. The company's equipment has been validated under existing frameworks and demonstrates that compliance can sometimes accelerate projects and reduce costs.
quotes: Without a national baseline, a technical requirement in one region will mean something materially different than in another, making compliance monitoring, lessons learned, and equipment design needlessly complex.
Some stakeholders have raised, and will continue to raise, concerns that these rules will make it difficult, expensive, or impossible for data centers to interconnect. We disagree.

Our equipment has been studied and approved for interconnection under ERCOT's interim large-load process pursuant to Senate Bill 6, and is designed to meet the pending Large Electronic Load ride-through requirements set out in NOGRR 282.

Our experience with other customers demonstrates that compliance can be achieved without significant delays or cost increases to the projects and, in some cases, can accelerate timelines and reduce project costs.

bins.name: Grid reliability and power quality risks
bins.desc: ON documents the unique power quality and stability risks posed by large computational loads through real grid events, technical mechanisms, and NERC findings. These include voltage instability, frequency interactions, and potential resonance-induced equipment damage that current grid design does not anticipate.
quotes: NERC's issuance of a Level 3 alert on large computational loads highlights the risks these large loads can introduce to the grid.
On July 10, 2024, a 230 kV lightning-arrestor failure in Northern Virginia produced six voltage drops; approximately 1.5 GW of data-center load disconnected near-simultaneously.
Synchronous generation cannot follow these ramps; the resulting voltage, frequency, and resonance interactions — including sub-synchronous oscillations — can damage equipment and threaten stability.
